## [03:01 - 06:06]

And I always say this, I'd rather have a new setter or I'd rather have a setter call one new lead seven times in the first two to three days, then call seven different leads from the CRM that are all two months old. ## [09:13 - 12:24]

First week or two, we kind of do a little bit of that. And then we really hone it down and we start getting stricter about their quality. One last thing, guys. Another big common pitfall is not just that the entrepreneur doesn't provide any curated opportunity volume for the setter, but they don't provide enough volume. So the rule of thumb we found will work really, really well. And this can be different for every company, but this is a great baseline in which you can start from and then test either way. Okay, is 500 to 800 low ticket buyers or opt-in leads per month per setter? So like we call opt-ins, we don't do low ticket. I've just found the opt-ins work just as well. So why add the added complexity of low ticket? So we have opt-ins and we do 500 to 800 opt-ins a month per setter. So each setter per month is usually going to get assigned 500 to 800 opt-ins and they're assigned immediately as they come in, which there's trainings on how to set that up later in the course. But 500 to 800 opt-ins per month per setter. And for low ticket stuff like $7, probably about the same ratio. Now, as you get higher prices for buyers, this number can probably go down. Okay, so for instance, you know, for about $47 buyers to $97 buyers, what I found is it's still about a 400 to 500 range at the minimum could be more, but that's probably about the minimum. Now, obviously, as it gets up to $297, $500, $1,000, $2,000 buyers, you know, this number could be drastically less. And you can have, you know, because the leads are going to be a little bit higher quality, also because they made a bigger purchase, they're going to be more receptive from somebody from your company actually reaching out and trying to get a hold of them. So just keep that in mind as the price goes up, this number and range is likely going to go down. Other thing is a, this is a great rule of thumb. Number one, the second rule of thumb I like is the one to one setter to closer ratio. So I found time and time again, just if you have six closers having six setters, usually is the perfect ratio. And we found anytime we, even if we have this figured out, if we go up to nine setters and six closers, typically the performance of the setters decreases and there's diminishing returns.

## [12:24 - 13:11]

The other thing is too, at that one to one ratio, you can usually get your setters pretty locked in at 20 to 30 sets per week, which is very good. As you start to get the setters too high in comparison to the closers, their performance goes down. Like for instance, I was working with a team very notable in the space, probably all you guys know they are, who had 15 setters and five closers. And the setters could like barely get 10 sets a week. And I was like, dude, you just got to fire a third of this team, keep the best guys. And once they did that, their performance went right back up. And they were actually getting the same amount of sets per week overall, their closers calendars were full, but the quality, the efficiency, all of that stuff went up. So this is a good rule of thumb as well. But that's it for this training guys for real now. And we'll see you in the next training.
